coredata.dc:title: Functionalized carbon dots for advanced batteries

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are a new class of carbon materials with ultrasmall size and unique physicochemical property. They have been widely studied since their discovery and have been gradually applied in various fields due to their quantum size, abundant surface functional groups, great biocompatibility, non-toxicity and photoluminescence characteristics. Recently, applications of carbon dots in the new generation batteries have also shown great potential. This review systematically summarized the research status of carbon dots in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), potassium ion batteries (PIBs), lithium-sulfur batteries (LSBs), etc., and discussed the applications of carbon dots in electrode materials, separator and electrolyte of advanced batteries in detail. Moreover, we also analyzed the challenges and development directions of carbon dots in batteries. This review aims to provide guidance and reference for the design and manufacture of the next generation of high-performance batteries.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are a new class of carbon materials with ultrasmall size and unique physicochemical property. They have been widely studied since their discovery and have been gradually applied in various fields due to their quantum size, abundant surface functional groups, great biocompatibility, non-toxicity and photoluminescence characteristics. Recently, applications of carbon dots in the new generation batteries have also shown great potential. This review systematically summarized the research status of carbon dots in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), potassium ion batteries (PIBs), lithium-sulfur batteries (LSBs), etc., and discussed the applications of carbon dots in electrode materials, separator and electrolyte of advanced batteries in detail. Moreover, we also analyzed the challenges and development directions of carbon dots in batteries. This review aims to provide guidance and reference for the design and manufacture of the next generation of high-performance batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is one of the most abundant elements in nature. Carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, have always been materials of great concern to researchers and have been widely used in multifarious fields. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon materials that stand out among the carbon family and are characterized by their quantum size, abundant surface functional groups, uniform dispersion, adjustable structure and composition, good biocompatibility, photoluminescent properties. In 2004, a group of fluorescent nanoparticles with a size of less than 18 nm were first found by Xu et al. [1] when purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes from arc smoke by electrophoresis. This new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial attracted more and more attention from researchers soon and a variety of synthesis methods for carbon dots have been dug out, as shown in Fig. 1 . In 2006, similar carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with a diameter of about 5 nm and enhanced fluorescence emission effect were prepared by Sun et al. [2]. They used argon as a carrier gas in laser ablation of carbon targets in the presence of water vapor, and then the products gone through some treatments including surface passivation and functionalization, and the CNPs with a new name of CDs could be obtained finally. After that, they conducted a more comprehensive study on nano CDs and found that surface passivation treatment could significantly improve the quantum yield of CDs, and the quantum yield of CDs after separation and purification by column chromatography was as high as 60%. [3] Later, CNPs were obtained through a simple combustion method by Liu et al. [4] for the first time in 2007, providing a new feasible method for the preparation of CDs. Then, Zhou et al. [5] reported a new method of the electrochemical method to prepare CDs. The electrochemical method to prepare CDs was realized by using 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate as electrolyte (dissolved in acetonitrile) and the carbon nanotube films as the negative electrode for multiple charge and discharge. Compared with laser ablation, electrochemical method has the advantages of controllable size and high purity. Subsequently, researchers conducted a more extensive study of carbon sources and methods for producing CDs, and more and more types of CDs were produced. CDs can be modified by hetero-atom doping [6,7] and surface modification [8] to improve the fluorescence characteristics and quantum yield. And CDs with different sizes and fluorescence characteristics can also be prepared by controlling reaction conditions. [9,10] At present, researchers are pursuing technical solutions that control the structure and morphology of CDs accurately, such as chiral CDs [11] and triangular CDs [12]. In addition, people also use biomass and other natural resources to prepare CDs. [13-15] This green and simple preparation method can reduce the consumption of chemicals and wastes, which is an important development direction of the economic preparation of CDs in the future (Table 1 ).

CDs were successfully applied to vivo biological imaging in 2009, due to their favorable biocompatibility and non-toxicity. [16] Subsequently, studies on drug delivery, [17] LEDs, [18] ion detection, [19] catalysis, [20-22] and other fields emerged in an endless stream. In recent years, applications of CDs in the field of energy storage and conversion have gradually become a hotspot, including capacitor, [23-25] solar cell, [26] Li/Na/K ion batteries, [27] and so on. Researchers have carried out a lot of studies on how to make full and effective use of the structural characteristics of carbon dots, how to control and construct high-performance composite materials with carbon dots, and how to use the unique properties of carbon dots to design advanced carbon materials (Fig. 2 ). Recently, researches in the field of energy storage show that both electrodes and electrolyte modified with CDs have significant improvements in coulombic efficiency, cyclic life, capacity, dendrite inhibition, etc.: (1) improving interface wettability and coulombic efficiency; (2) promoting the conductivity of electrode materials and the rate performance; (3) enhancing structural stability and extending cyclic life; (4) adjusting the morphology and structure of electrode materials to provide better lithium/sodium/potassium storage performance; (5) providing initial nucleation sites for lithium metals and inducing uniform lithium deposition; (6) promoting the ORR/OER (oxygen reduction reaction/oxygen evolution reaction) performances of metal-air batteries. [28,29]

About the reported reviews of carbon dots, most are around the carbon dots in synthesis methods, fluorescence analysis, biological imaging, and photoelectric devices. This review mainly summarized and discussed the research progress of carbon dots in terms of advanced batteries, including lithium/sodium/potassium ion batteries, lithium-sulfur batteries, metal-air batteries, and detailedly discussed the application progress of carbon dots in derivative carbon materials, surface modification and morphology control of electrode in the meantime. This review aims to provide a certain reference and basis for the application of carbon dots in advanced batteries.

There has been no unified regulation on the naming and classification of carbon dots due to the large differences between carbon dots obtained from different methods. Recently, some scholars have classified carbon dots into four types based on the difference in carbon cores (Fig. 3 ): [30] graphene quantum dots (GQDs), [31,32] carbon quantum dots (CQDs), [33] carbon nanodots (CNDs), [34] and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). [35,36] graphene quantum dots refer to single-layer or multilayer graphene fragments with functional groups on the edges or inner layer defects, which have unique quantum confinement effects and edge effects. And the surface size of GQDs is usually less than 10 nm, and the thickness is generally less than 5 layers of graphene. The quantum confinement effect of GQDs not only refers to the confinement effect of its size but also includes the confinement effect of the conjugated π system in which in-plane defects are isolated. Generally, increasing the content of oxygen functional groups in GQDs will increase the number of defects, resulting in more conjugated π systems that can be used as fluorescence centers. Carbon nanodots or carbon quantum dots are generally spherical-like carbon nanomaterials with a surface rich in functional groups and a diameter of less than 10 nm. They exhibit eigenstate luminescence and size-based quantum confinement effect, and adjusting their size and functional group can realize the adjustment of photoluminescence wavelength. However, there are also many studies that refer to carbon dots other than graphene quantum dots as carbon quantum dots. And some researchers stated that carbon nanodots are spherical carbon dots with amorphous carbon core while carbon quantum dots mainly refer to spherical carbon dots with crystal carbon core. Carbonized polymer dots are carbonized by polymer nanodots, some of which are incompletely carbonized and part of the polymer chain remains. These classifications are based on the different understandings of carbon dots, especially there is no unified understanding of the classification of carbon dots other than graphene quantum dots, which does not affect the development of carbon dots. In addition, CDs are also known as carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), [4]polymer carbon nanodots (PCNDs), [37]carbogenic dots [38] and carbon nanocrystals(CNCs) [39,40] in some papers.

As shown in Fig. 4 , CDs consist of two parts: carbon core and surface passivation layer. [41] The carbon core constitutes the skeleton of CDs, which is generally smaller than 10 nm. It can be not only the sp2 graphene fragments, but also the carbon nanodots composed of sp2 and sp3 carbon, or even the polymer dots constructed by non-conjugated polymers. CDs have a larger specific surface area, more unsaturated bonds and less coordination of surface phase atoms. Hence, CDs will bond to other groups to form a surface passivation layer in order to reduce the Gibbs free energy of the system. If there are no additional surface passivation introduced, the surface atoms of CDs are connected to a large quantities of oxygen-containing functional groups generally, mainly hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. [4,42] Bonding other passivation modifiers on the surface of CDs, such as oligomers, polymers or biomolecules, will further improve their polarity, optical properties and chemical activity. [35]

There are various methods to synthesize carbon dots, which can be divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods according to carbon sources, as shown in Fig. 5 . [43] Then, crude products are purified by dialysis, centrifugation, electrophoresis, column chromatography, and so on. The carbon sources to prepare carbon dots by the “top-down” method are generally carbon nanotubes, [5,44] graphite, [45] carbon fibers, [46,47] graphene oxide [48] and candle soot, [4]etc. And then these carbon-rich substances are cut and stripped by arc discharge, [1] laser ablation, [2] electrochemical synthesis, [47,49] and so on, and CDs are formed finally. However, carbon sources for “bottom-up” approaches of carbon dots are mostly small organic molecules or oligomers such as citric acid, [50,51] glucose, [52] polyethylene glycol, [53] ionic liquid, [54] amino acids [11,55,56] and even a variety of biomass, like soybean milk, [34] bagasse, [57] peels, [58] leaves, [59,60] coffee grounds, [61] etc. These carbon dots are finally formed through the process of dehydration, condensation and carbonization. These common methods mainly include combustion, [4] hydrothermal/solvothermal reaction, [37] chemical oxidation, [62] microwave [52,63] and template method [64,65]. However, the two synthetic methods have their own advantages and disadvantages. The “top-down” method is to produce carbon dots by smashing the skeleton completely, which has low quantum yields, while the “bottom-up” method uses small organic molecules as carbon sources to synthesize carbon dots, but only when the carbon source is small molecules of organic materials, this method can obtain a high quantum yield.

The synthesis methods as well as carbon sources of carbon dots are various, and their chemical structure varies with different synthesis methods. In general, carbon cores of CDs prepared by etching and stripping methods with graphite or graphite-like carbon sources have a graphite microcrystalline structure. For example, GQDs have a single or several graphene layers with bonded chemical groups on the edges and they have a certain degree of crystallinity due to the presence of carbon nuclei. [66] CDs prepared by microwave or hydrothermal methods from small organic molecules sometimes are amorphous carbon or polymer aggregates. Among them, CNDs are usually spherical and can be divided into CNPs without lattice and CQDs with obvious lattice, but this is not universally unchangeable, some researchers called all CNDs with the size of less than 10 nm as CQDs; and PDs are polymers that are polymerized/assembled or cross-linked by nonconjugated polymers. Different researchers have different opinions on the classification of carbon dots, the classification of carbon dots is only for a better study of carbon dots, and the specific classification method does not affect the development of carbon dots. The surface groups of CDs can be directly introduced in the preparation process, or they can be bonded to various organic functional groups through subsequent passivation treatment. The surface of CDs obtained from “bottom-up” approaches is usually passivated by precursor molecules, while the surface of CDs from “top-down” approaches is dominated by oxygen-containing functional groups. If the precursors or auxiliary agents with heteroatoms (N, S, B, P, etc.) are introduced in the CDs preparation process, the atomic doping of CDs can be realized, which can effectively regulate the electronic surface state and local chemical characteristics of carbon nanomaterials. [67]

With the wide applications of carbon dots, higher requirements are put forward for the preparation of carbon dots. Although CDs can be synthesized by multiple carbon sources through multiple methods, the quantum yields of CDs synthesized by most methods are relatively low, so it is still a challenge to expand the synthesis scale of CDs. Mai et al. [68] used glycerol as a high boiling point solvent, so that the mixture of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) can be used to synthesize CQDs of more than 10 g in a few minutes by microwave method, and the purified CQDs were spherical and 2–8 nm in diameter. Our group synthesized CQDs from acetaldehyde or acetone in NaOH solution and realized the large-scale preparation of carbon dots with 2–5 nm in size. [69,70] However, the large-scale preparation methods of other types of CDs remain to be studied. On the other hand, the synthesis of CDs with excellent performance is also a difficulty, such as the strong and wide light absorption performance for photoelectric devices, adjustable energy structure for catalysts and controllable surface groups in sensing and energy storage. Moreover, there are still many basic problems that have not been studied in detail, such as nucleation and growth process of CDs, and the influences of synthetic methods and conditions of CDs on their structure, performances, morphology and size control and luminescence mechanism. Nevertheless, the existence of CDs in different systems also makes it difficult to provide a consistent structure and morphology regulation mechanism. Therefore, it is of great significance to reveal the relationship between the composition and properties of CDs. [30]

CDs can be used as advanced electrode materials, and their surface groups and structures are particularly important. For instance, the amino group makes a great contribution to the pseudo-capacitance, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are helpful to improve the wettability between electrode and electrolyte and the quantum size CDs can also adjust the structure of metal oxide. [28,71] In addition, CDs can coordinate with some metal ions through their plentiful surface functional groups, thus exerting a vital influence on the morphology and particle size of the final products. Furthermore, functional CDs have good water solubility or oil solubility when introduced with different groups, and are also great separator modifier and electrolyte additives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots in batteries
ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous consumption of traditional non-renewable fossil energy, energy crisis and environmental pollution is becoming more and more serious, so the development of new renewable energy has attracted more attention. [72] Under this background, electrochemical energy storage technologies, like rechargeable batteries or Capacitors have become an important development direction of energy storage systems due to its characteristics of safety, low cost and high efficiency. [73] For the past few years, rechargeable lithium-ion battery technologies have been massively used in electric vehicles, portable electronic equipment and energy storage systems. With the successful practical applications of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), studies on similar secondary rechargeable batteries, including sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), potassium ion batteries (PIBs), lithium-sulfur batteries (Li-S batteries), etc., emerged one after another. The actual electrochemical performances of rechargeable batteries depend largely on their electrode materials. Therefore, multifarious high-capacity anode materials have been explored, including carbon-based materials, [74] titanium-based materials (such as Li4Ti5O12, [75] TiO2 [76-78]), alloy-based materials (such as Si, [79] NiSb, [80] Sb [81-84]), metal oxide/sulfide materials (such as Mn3O4, [85] MoS2, [86] Sb2S3 [87]) and so on.

However, these materials have their own inherent defects. Inspired by the applications of carbon dots in supercapacitors, researchers began to apply multifunctional carbon dots in battery systems from 2014, [88] as shown in Fig. 6 . It was certified that electrode materials modified with CDs were effective methods to solve many problems, such as increasing electronic conductivity and improving ion transmission and adjusting the size and morphology of metal and metal oxides. In addition to electrode materials, the separators and electrolyte also have a high impact on the electrochemical performance of batteries. Surprisingly, CDs can also be used as modifiers for separators to accelerate ion transmission and inhibit the formation of dendrites and as electrolyte additives for lithium-sulfur batteries to inhibit the dissolution and shuttle effect of polysulfide and induce uniform lithium deposition.

Lithium-ion batteries have the advantages of high voltage, low self-discharge and high energy density, but their low power density and inferior cycling life are key issues for their applications in electric vehicles (EVs) and other equipment. [89] The energy density, power density and cycling life of lithium-ion batteries mainly depend on their electrodes, and the slow diffusion control process and structural stability of the electrodes are their main concerns. [90,91] In recent decades, the demand for electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles has become increasingly urgent, which has greatly promoted the development of LIBs with high reversible capacity, excellent rate performance and long-cycle stability. Researchers have made great efforts in the progress of new electrode materials. But they are limited to selecting appropriate materials, composition adjustment and proper structural design. As emerging materials that can modify electrode materials of LIBs, CDs are produced endless research fruits in recent years.

Regarding the applications of CDs in cathode materials, they are mainly used as surface coatings. Vanadium dioxide (VO2) has always been supposed to an ideal electrode material of LIBs due to its large specific capacity, low cost and luxuriant sources. However, the charge transfer resistance will increase rapidly during the cycling progress and the surface modification of CDs can ensure the high surface conductivity and structural integrity of VO2.

Chao et al. [92] designed a VO2 nanoribbon array supported by a 3D graphene foam (GF) skeleton and coated with GQDs (GVG, Fig. 7 a-c), which showed a great rate performance and excellent cycling stability in both LIBs and SIBs. The as-designed electrodes realized bicontinuous electron and lithium/sodium iontransfer channels through the GQD-GF network without the need for supererogatory conductive additives (Fig. 7d). And the electrolyte can enter the interval between nanoarrays on both the outside and inner surface of GF during the lithiation process, allowing Li+ and electrons to contact the VO2 nanoarrays directly. The GQDs coating improves ion diffusion and charges transport kinetics and the uniform GQDs coating can also restrain the dissolution of VO2 and agglomeration of the array. The discharge capacity of GVG in LIBs is 421, 390, 198, and 151 mAh g−1 at 1/3, 1, 60, and 120 C, respectively, which is more favorable than that of GV without GQDs. After charging and discharging at high rate, the capacity of GVG can recover to 389 mA g−1 when the current density returns to 1 C. In addition, the capacity retention rate of GVG is 94% at 60 C (18 A g−1) after 1500 cycles, and its coulombic efficiency is about 100%, showing superior cycling stability.

Balogun et al. [93] used a simple way to prepare VO2 nanowires directly grown on the three-dimensional carbon cloth, and followed by immersing them in the CQDs solution for surface engineering (C-VOCQD, Fig. 7f-h). The CQDs on the surface of the interlaced nanowires not only make it easier and faster to transport ions but also can act as sensitizers and protective agents to protect the interwoven nanowires from agglomeration, which makes VO2 nanowires excellent structural stability. C-VOCQD cathode shows a satisfactory discharge capacity of 402 mAh g−1 at 0.3 C when it is used in LIBs and can deliver a capacity of 168 mAh g−1 even at 60 C, exhibiting higher capacity and better rate performance than the C-VO electrode without CQDs.

Zhang and coworkers [94] constructed a unique self-supported, binder-free iron fluoride vertical nanosheets array by directly grown iron fluoride vertical nanosheets array on treated titanium foil, followed with electrophoresis GQDs to surface modification (GQDs@FFNA). Not only can the GQDs modification increase the entire electrical conductivity of the electrode, but also can possess a protective effect on the nanosheets structure. In this work, GQDs were introduced to FeF3•0.33H2O material for the first time, and tremendously enhance its electrochemical performance. By the synergistic effect of thevertical nanosheets array and GQDs modification, the GQDs@FFNA cathode can achieve superior rate performance and ultra-long cycleperformance. The capacity of 161.5 mAh g−1 and 117 mAh g−1 at current densities of 1 C (1 C = 200 mA g−1) and 20 C can be obtained respectively and the capacity decay rate is as low as 0.03% per cycle during 1000 cycles at 2 C.

Surface modification of the cathode materials with conductive CDs, such as GQDs and graphite-type CQDs, can improve the conductivity and improve the electrochemical reaction kinetics significantly. In addition, CDs can also prevent the nanowires from agglomerating to ensure the structural stability of electrode materials, so that better rate performance and cycle stability can be obtained.

So far, graphite is still the dominant anode material for commercial LIBs. Graphite has the advantages of abundant resources, low cost and great conductivity, but its low diffusion coefficient of lithium ion and low specific capacity will limit its application in high energy and power density LIBs. Besides, its low working potential is less than 0.2 V (as compared to Li/Li+) may lead to the growth of lithium dendrites on the surface of anode under overcharge. CDs, as a member of carbon nanomaterials, can also be used as carbon-based electrode materials theoretically. Javed et al. [95] first used CQDs (Fig. 8 a) obtained from glucose as anode materials of LIBs directly. From the TEM image in Fig. 8b, it is distinct that CQDs are monodispersed spherical particles of narrow size distribution in the diametric range of 3–5 nm. Most of CQDs in Fig. 8c show evident parallel planes thatdemonstrate their crystalline nature, in which the lattice spacing of 3.2 Å refers to the (002) plane in the graphitic structure, while a small number of amorphous particles were also seen. These CQDs show great cycling stability and excellent lithium/sodium storage performance, which certifies the potential of CQDs for LIBs. When they are used as the anode of LIBs, the high capacity of 864.9 mAh g−1 can be gained at 0.5 C after 500 cycles, and the capacity of 340.2 mAh g−1 can also be acquired at the high rate of 20 C after 500 cycles. This unexceptionable electrochemical performance is due to the fact that the appropriate layer spacing of 3.2 Å can provide insertion sites for Li+ (0.76 Å). Furthermore, the quantum size of CQDs can reduce ion-diffusion length and provide continuous transmission channels, so that the CQDs can deliver a high lithium storage capacity. This study is of great importance for a basic understanding of the conductive properties of CQDs, so that further improvements can be made to regulate the electrochemistry of the conductive composites carefully.

Using methane as the carbon source, Saroja et al. [96] simply synthesized GQDs, boron-doped (B-GQDs) and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) (Fig. 8d) through a chemical vapor deposition technology and studied the lithium/sodium ion storage characteristics of these doped and undoped GQDs. Morphological of the prepared GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD are analyzed by TEM images in Fig. 8e-g that illustrate their uniform distribution. The B-GQDs, as the anodes of LIBs, can deliver a high capacity of 1097 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1. Moreover, B-GQDs have satisfactory cycling performance and a good capacity retention rate of 95.7% after 500 cycles. Detailed studies show that the presence of edge defects in GQDs helps to adsorb moremetal ions and doping helps to strengthen the insertion process, which is responsible for their superb specific capacity among the carbon-based materials. This research thus provides a new strategy for the development of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures and provides new scope for the progress of the new type of anode materials for LIBs. Wang et al. [97] achieved a sustainable and green method to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs from egg yolk (N-CDs, Fig. 8h-k) with high relative QY. These CDs have favorable stability and fluorescence characteristics. After carbonization, they are used as anodes of LIBs, showing great electrochemical performance (Fig. 8l,m). It has been proved that CDs and CDs doped with heteroatoms are nice anode materials for LIBs, but the large-scale preparation is the main problem that needs to be solved urgently at present. So that this study can be a reference in the respect of the fabrication and compositing of carbon-based electrodes from active and promising CDs.

Porous carbon materials with high specific surface area and adjustable pore structure and volume have drawn extensive attention in the area of energy storage. Porous carbon materials can be prepared by pyrolyzing polymers or made from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with various shapes and structures. Their holes can provide channels to accelerate ions transportation and they can transport electrons through-hole walls. These porous carbon materials have high rate performance generally, but their poor conductivity and instability still suppress their applications in batteries. In order to solve these problems, Yu et al. [98] combined porous carbon materials derived from metal-organic frameworks (ZIF-8) with GQDs, and prepared the porous carbon composite materials named C(ZIF-8)@GQDs by in situ self-assembly method. These composite materials have a high specific surface area of 668 m2 g−1 and contain many micropores and mesopores, which can supply more active sites for electrochemical reactions and increase the contact area between electrode and electrolyte. The addition of GQDs greatly improves the degree of graphitization and makes materials have better electrochemical performance. The discharge capacity of C(ZIF-8)@GQDs is as high as 708 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1. Moreover, C(ZIF-8)@GQDs can provide a capacity of 493 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 after 200 cycles, while pure C(ZIF-8) can only maintain a specific capacity of 329 mAh g−1, proving that GQDs coating plays a significant role in improving the electrochemical performance of electrode materials. On the other hand, a kind of rice husk-based activated carbon materials modified with GQDs that are functionalized by phenylalanine (RHAC-GQDs) was prerared by Li et al. [99] The charge transfer resistance of the electrode was reduced from 577.7 Ω to 123.9 Ω after the introduction of GQDs, and the diffusion coefficient of lithium ion was increased by 175 times. At the same time, GQDs also played an important role in improving the cycling stability of the batteries. These two studies prove that GQDs can improve the electrochemical performance of carbon-based materials effectively, indicating that carbon materials modified with GQDs have broad prospects for LIBs.

As materials of lithium storage, titanium-based materials have received great attention from the scientific community because of their excellent cycling stability, safety and small volume expansion. Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) is supposed to be the most promising anode material for LIBs. LTO anode has the advantages of great insertion/extraction reversibility of lithium ion, small volume and structure changes during charge and discharge and flat potential platform. However, its main disadvantages are its inherent low electronic conductivity and diffusion coefficient of lithium ion. Recently, researchers have tried several strategies to improve the electrochemical performance of LTO successfully, consisting of the introduction of conductive phases, like metals or carbon materials, control of morphology and ion doping. Researches on mixing and coating graphene on LTO crystals have achieved great results and GQDs have additional specific properties with excellent dispersancy and unique quantum sizes, which make them superior conductive additives and structural supports for LTO.

Li et al. [100] successfully synthesized a new type oflithium titanate/nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots material (LTO/N,S-GQDs) that gave a fully crystalline interconnected porous framework consisted of nanoscale LTO crystals (Fig. 9 a). Its unique structure and the introduction of N,S-GQDs created both ultrafast electron transfer and electrolyte transport and improved specificcapacity. The discharge specific capacity of LTO/N, S-GQDs can reach 254.2 mAh g−1 and 126.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and 10 C (1 C = 175 mA g−1), respectively. Furthermore, its specific capacity still remained 180.5 mAh g−1 at 2 C after 2000 cycles with the capacity retention rate of 96.9%, which proved that the LTO/N,S-GQDs electrode had great rate performance and excellent cycle stability. Similarly, Khan et al. [101] coated LTO with an ultrathin protective and charge-transport layer of N-GQDs (LTO-NGQ) too. N-GQDs induced a dense structure via charge interaction with LTO and Super P, which offered a shorter channel for electron transfer (Fig. 9b). The specific capacity of the LTO-NGQ20 electrode was about 161 mAh g−1 at 50 C and it maintained more than 500 cycles. And excellent cyclic performance and long cyclic life of LTO-NGQ20 at a high current dencity were also obtained successfully. In the process of the reaction between LTO and the electrolyte of LiPF6, on the surface of the LTO was formed an SEI film consisted of decomposition products (such as Li2CO3, ROCOOLi, and LiF) accompanied with the evolution of gaseous species, which play an significant role in the electrochemical performance of the batteries. In the course of the reaction, the phase of the external surface of the Li4Ti5O12 also transformed. When it was at the high current density, the SEI layer was not enough to prevent the external surface of the Li4Ti5O12 from decomposition. Therefore, the quantity of reaction products, the thickness of the SEI layer, and the amount of produced gas increased. However, after coating N-GQDs on the surface of Li4Ti5O12, this phenomenon was sharply decreased, as shown in Fig. 9b. In conclusion, N-GQDs as an efficient protective layer has the following characteristics: (1) it can prevent LTO from degrading in the air; (2) a gauzy and slippy solid electrolyte interface layer is formed on the surface of LTO, which serves as a charge transfer layer; (3) it can inhibit the surface reaction between LTO and electrolyte; (4) it can also suppress the gas generation during charge and discharge progresses.

To improve the specific capacity and conductivity of LTO, Nan and coworkers [102] synthesized CQDs and Al3+/Mn4+ co-doped LTO composites (LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs) to enhance its electrical properties (Fig. 9c). The LTO-Al/Mn-CQDs composites improved the capacity of LIBs and charge and discharge speed successfully. Their specific capacity in the first cycle reached 296.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C, the impedance was reduced to 16.8 Ω, and the capacity was maintained at 236 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles. In addition, LTO quantum dots uniformly dispersed on a three-dimensional carbon skeleton derived from carbon dots were designed by our group (LTO QDs/CFs, Fig. 9d). [103] The composite materials can reach a discharge capacity of 191 mAh g−1 and 115.4 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C and 40 C respectively. Even if the current density returns from 40 C to 0.5 C, the capacity can still be restored to 169.1 mAh g−1, and the capacity retention rate can reach 93.87% at 1 C after 200 cycles. The electrochemical performance of LTO QDs/CFs is significantly better than that of unmodified LTO, which proves that carbon dots-derived carbon materials can also be utilized as a carbon matrix to enhance the electrochemical performance of LTO.

Similar to LTO, TiO2with different crystalline has the advantages of environmental friendliness, safety, and chemical stability. However, the practical applications of TiO2 anode are limited by its low conductivity, slow ion-diffusion rate, and high resistance in the interface of electrode and electrolyte at high rates, resulting in a low charge and discharge capacity and poor rate performance. The common strategies to enhance the electrochemical performance of TiO2 are to design the nanostructure of TiO2 and form the composite with carbon materials. [104-106] Inspired by a series of unique physical and chemical properties result from doping/modifying/implanting CDs into the matrix of TiO2, [49] Ming et al. [107] first introduced CDs into the anode material of the battery system and obtained enhanced electrochemical performance of TiO2, and they comparatively studied and discussed the influence of CDs insertion and porosity on the electrochemical performance of TiO2. Interestingly, unlike the traditional surface coating with carbon materials, or loading it into the carbon matrix, embedding CDs into the structure of TiO2 could accelerate the transfer capability of electrons from the internal body to the surface of TiO2. They demonstrated that embedding CDs into TiO2 could reasult in a change in the structure. Besides, the CDs embedded in the structure of TiO2 can react with TiO2 to form a large number of chemical bonds, such as Ti-O-C and Ti-C-O, which can reduce the redox potential of TiO2 and introduce more active centers that are beneficial to lithium ion storage.

Also, combining with highly conductive materials or doping with N and B or other heteroatoms can improve the electronic conductivity of TiO2. Zhang and coworkers [108] successfully designed and fabricated hierarchically structural TiO2-x embedded with GQDs (TiO2-x/GQDs, Fig. 10 a,b). The introduction of GQDs is not only conducive to electron transfer, but also has a certain impact on narrowing the bandgap of TiO2 that is Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies, which can cause changes in the electronic structure of metal oxides and the energetics for electron and ion transportation. Moreover, GQDs are confined in the pores, which can increase the specific surface area of the electrode materials, thus increasing the contact area between the electrolyte and active materials and improving their conductivity. Therefore, TiO2-x/GQDs anodes show remarkable electrochemical performances compared with the pristine rutile TiO2 anode and they can deliver a superior lithium storage capacity of 168.5 mAh g−1 at 5 C and long-term cyclability of 160.1 mAh g−1 at 10 C after 500 cycles (Fig. 10c) when applied as the anode in LIBs.

The combination of N-doping and CDs modification can greatly improve the conductivity of TiO2 materials, and CDs can also control the nanostructure of N-TiO2. Our group [109]combined the merits of CDs, doping and nanostructures to design a new composite of N-doped TiO2 nanorods decorated with CDs (N-TiO2/C-dots, Fig. 10d,e). Fig. 10f and Fig. 10g show the galvanostatic charge/discharge curves and cyclic performance of the N–TiO2/C-dots electrodes in LIBs, respectively. The as-prepared N-TiO2/C-dots composites utilized as anode materials for LIBs are found to maintain a capacity of 185 mAh g−1with the capacity retention of 91.6% even at a high rate of 10 C (1 C = 168 mA g−1) over 1000 cycles. Similarly, the N-TiO2/C-dots composites have gained outstanding cycling stability and excellent rate performance as anodes of SIBs. In general, the electrochemical performances of N-TiO2/C-dots composites for LIBs and SIBs are much higher than that of pure N-TiO2, which is attributed to the decorating C-dots and N-doping. The conductivity of the N-TiO2/C-dots composites measured directly is about 10 times larger than that of the pure N-TiO2 and the impedance of the pure N-TiO2 is also greater than that of the N-TiO2/C-dots composites, which proves that the addition of CDs and N-doping can improve the electrical conductivity and charge transfer kinetics of the anodes.

The titanium-based materials modified with CDs can improve the inherent defects of the materials itself, and they are very likely to be put into practical applications in the near future as materials with remarkable cycling stability. Modification with CDs will also be an effective way to improve the electrochemical performance of titanium-based materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs in metal compound anode materials
ce:section.ce:para: In 2000, Tarascon [110] pointed out that transition metal oxides can develop into anode material candidates with high-capacity through the conversion reaction mechanism (MOx + 2 xLi ↔ M + xLi2O). However, the low conductivity and volume expansion of transition metal oxides are the main problems in their commercialization, and their composition, structure and morphology have different effects on capacity and capacity retention. In order to make metal oxides useful for LIBs, they must be well designed for their composition, structure and morphology.

Among metal oxide anode materials, CuO has the superiorities of a high theoretical capacity of 674 mAh g−1, environmental friendliness and low cost as anode for LIBs. Whereas, its bulky volume expansion (174%), low conductivity and subsequent particle crushing are still key issues that need to be resolved. Herein, Nanostructure, heteros-doping and surface coating are the main methods to solve the defects of CuO. Zhu et al. [111] designed and prepared CuO+Cu+GQDs triaxial nanowire arrays for performance enhancement as LIB anode (CCG, Fig. 11 a-d) successfully using GQDs as the CuO coating materials for the first time. GQDs coating on the CuO/Cu surface may have acted as a mechanically soft and electrically conductivity “armor” that can lower the electrode polarization and increase the charge collection efficiency, and guard the structural integrity of each CuO nanowire as well. Besides, the GQD layer can also make for a high initial coulombic efficiency of 87% by preventing the thick SEI layer on pure CuO/Cu. In consequently, the CCG anode delivered excellent rate performance with an initial discharge capacity of about 960 mAh g−1 and cycling stability with a high reversible capacity of 780 mAh g−1 at 1/3 C (1 C = 674 mA g−1) after 500 cycles.

NiO has a high theoretical capacity of 718 mAh g−1, but its structure is unstable during cycling processes, so it is necessary to design the unique structure and modify the surface. Yin et al. [112] used a combination of structural design and outer support to modify the yolk-shell NiO microspheres with two functionalized GQDs (NiO/GQDs, Fig. 11e) successfully. In addition to the advantages of yolk-shell morphology that furnishes NiO/GQDs admirable structural features to reduce internal stress, the excellent performance of NiO/GQDs is mainly attributed to the modification of GQDs. GQDs on the surface can act as not only the conducting intermediary to strengthen electron transfer, but also the toughening additives to buffer the volume changes during cycling. Furthermore, GQDs with carboxyl/amino groups can further accelerate the diffusion of lithium ions and be introduced into microspheres as outer support for high-performance lithium storage. The prepared NiO/GQDs-COOH and NiO/GQDs-NH2 electrodes displayed enhanced lithium storage performance with a capacity of 1081 mAh g−1 and 834 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 250 cycles (Fig. 11f), respectively. while the capacity of bald NiO without modification of GQDs obviously decrease to 396 mAh g−1 after cycling, which proved that the modification of GQDs on the NiO surface can greatly improve its long-cycle stability.

Mn3O4 with a high theoretical specific capacity of 937 mAh g−1 and stable spinel crystal structure is also a promising anode material for LIBs. The Mn3O4 composite materials modified with CDs (Mn3O4/Cdots) were first prepared by our group through a green electrochemical method. [113] SEM and TEM images of Mn3O4/Cdots sample in Fig. 11h-k clearly present that the composites are composed of octahedral Mn3O4 particles that are induced by CDs, which can improve their electrochemical performance significantly. The electrochemical mechanism of pure Mn3O4 and Mn3O4/Cdots composite is shown in Fig. 11g. Mn atoms are primarily oxidized to high valence Mn oxide (MnOx) films by the anodic process. And then MnOx can be reduced via the cathodic process (I) and converted to stable Mn3O4 nanocrystals in NaCl solution. When CDs were introduced in NaCl solution as reductants in the process of electrochemical exfoliation (II), the MnOx will be reduced via the cathodic process or C reduction process. As a result, the reduction process is promoted, which can be beneficial to facilitate crystal growth and form a stable Mn3O4 octahedral structure. The initial coulombic efficiency of Mn3O4/Cdots electrodes is higher than that of pristine Mn3O4, because the octahedral Mn3O4 in the Mn3O4/Cdots composites is of great benefit to decrease the charge transfer resistance with alternating Mn and O atom layers structure. In addition, the discharge capacity of the Mn3O4/Cdots composites is 934 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 after 50 cycles, which is almost 5 times higher than that of pure Mn3O4 electrode, showing better cyclic stability (Fig. 11l). This work proved that CDs not only can significantly improve the conductivity of electrodes, but also can be used as a new kind of surfactant to control the morphology of products, providing a new application of carbon dots in electrode materials.

Fe2O3 can also be used as anode material for LIBs, and it has received extensive attention due to its environmentally friendly, abundant resources and low cost. Zhang et al. [114] prepared a self-supporting and binder-free C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite (Fig. 12 a) via two-step electrodeposition, using a unique spatial structure of nano-Fe2O3 as well as an electrically conductive network with the “bridge effect” of C-GQDs. The C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite has a sheet-like and antler-like skeleton structure (Fig. 12b,c) that has enough three-dimensional pores to buffer volume expansion and a protective layer of C-GQDs that can help to maintain electrode integrity. This composite electrode can also solve the problems of hindered ion transfer, reduced electric conductivity, and obvious capacity decay because of the addition of a binder during the construction of conventional electrodes. The electrochemical analysis shows that the specific capacity of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite materials can reach 1582.5 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 and the specific capacitance is still as high as 1320 mAh g−1 after 110 cycles (Fig. 12d). In addition, the capacity can reach 1091 mAh g−1 even at a high current density of 5 A g−1, showing satisfactory rate performance.

Nb2O5 has attracted more and more attention on account of its high safety and good structural stability as anode material for LIBs. Lin et al. [115] synthesized a Nb2O5/carbon nanocomposite in which the Nb2O5 homogeneously distribute in a carbon matrix derived from CQDs that can both increase the electronic conductivity and reduce the size of Nb2O5 crystal and prevent the aggregation of Nb2O5 during cycling. Compared with pure Nb2O5 electrode, the as-prepared Nb2O5/carbon composite exhibited higher specific capacity, more excellent rate capability and better cyclic stability as anode for LIBs. Similarly, bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) is also a potential anode material for LIBs and it can also be modified with carbon quantum dots. Prasath et al. [116] synthesized a Bi2O3/carbon nanocomposite that consisted of uniform spherical particles of Bi2O3 with sparsely intertwined CQDs. As anode material for LIBs, CQD-Bi2O3 composite displayed great electrochemical activity and delivers an initial discharge capacity as high as 1500 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C, and it maintains 1200 mAh g−1 for the next three cycles, which is higher than previously reported Bi2O3, Bi2O3/rGO and 3D OM β-Bi2O3 anode materials. [117,118]

Binary transition metal oxides as anode materials for LIBs have been extensively studied due to their great electrochemical activity, more redox reactions and significant synergistic effects between two different metals, and the assistance of GQDs coating can improve their electrochemical performance effectively. As shown in Fig. 12e, Wu et al. [119] synthesized yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO microspheres with the surface decoration of carboxyl-functionalized GQDs (Co3O4@CuO@GQDs) and investigated their lithium storage performance. The yolk-shell Co3O4@CuO structure extracted from MOFs exhibits well-defined microstructure and high porosities and the unique structure is aimed to accommodate the stepwise lithium insertion mechanism (first in the CuO shell and then in the Co3O4 core), which helps to mitigate structural changes during cycling. The GQDs embellished on the surface of Co3O4@CuO microspheres can not only provide larger specific surface area, more active centers and improved electronic conductivity, but also work as a buffer to assuage the volume expansion and a reservoir for the electrolyte to increase the ionic conductivity effectively. In addition, the carboxyl groups of GQDs exhibit commendable hydrophilicity that is beneficial for the assembly of GQDs with Co3O4@CuO and also shows a strong affinity to Li+. On the basis of the above advantages, the Co3O4@CuO@GQDs anodes deliver an initial capacity of 816 mAh g−1 and a high reversible capacity of 1054 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 200 cycles with a stable spherical morphology of the electrodes. However, the capacity of Co3O4@CuO microspheres without GQDs suffers from a sharp capacity decline after 200 cycles (Fig. 12f), which proves that the Co3O4@CuO electrodes modified with GQDs can display more satisfactory structural stability and cycling stability. In a similar way, multilayered cobalt-nickel bimetal oxide hollow spheres modified with GQDs (NiO@Co3O4@GQDs) were designed and prepared by Yin et al., [120] as shown in Fig. 12g-i. The NiO@Co3O4@GQDs hybrid is also based on a stepwise lithium storage mechanism (first in the Co3O4 shell, then in the NiO core), and the carboxylated GQDs can promote the ion/electron transportation and increase the specific surface area towards improved electrochemical performance. As the anode for LIBs, the NiO@Co3O4@GQDs electrodes exhibited a large reversible capacity of 1158 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 250 cycles (Fig. 12j) and a capacity of 803 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 after 500 cycles, which showed superior cycling performance and excellent rate properties.

Metal sulfide is another important anode material based on conversion reaction and its energy storage mechanism is similar to that of metal oxide. Better thermal stability, conductivity and higher theoretical capacity make it a good choice for LIBs anode, but capacity degradation is one of the key problems of metal sulfides. The theoretical specific capacity of molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) is about 670 mAh g−1, and the aggregation behavior of MoS2 and the formation of polymer intermediates during cycling can reduce its specific capacity that can be improved by adjusting the number of layers, particle size and morphology during the synthesis process. Guo et al. [121] first synthesized MoS2 nanosheets modified with GQDs simply by the solvothermal method to improve the lithium storage performance of MoS2. Compared with the pristine MoS2, the obtained GQDs/MoS2 materials displayed significantly improved electrochemical performance. The initial discharge capacity of the GQDs/MoS2 materials with a GQDs content of 10.5% can reach 1393 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and it remains at 1031 mAh g−1 after 80 cycles, and the capacity can reach 660 mAh g−1 even at a high current density of 5 A g−1. The improved performance of MoS2 is mainly assigned to the following reasons: (1) GQDs can increase the electrical conductivity of composite materials, thereby promoting the charge transfer; (2) A appropriate amount of GQDs doping can act as a binder to improve the cyclic stability; (3) GQDs can expand the interlayer spacing of MoS2 nanosheets and boost the intercalation of Li+. Moreover, the highly reversible redox reaction of Mo has been achieved in the present composite electrode materials owing to the doping of GQDs. However, too much or too little GQDs content in MoS2 materials will lead to poor electrochemical performance, so that the appropriate amount of GQDs in composite materials needs to be continuously explored in experiments. Furthermore, Riyanto et al. [122] prepared another kind of MoS2 materials modified with boron-doped GQDs (BGQS/MoS2) as anode material for LIBs, which showed similar results.

In general, when carbon dots modify the metal compound materials, they can adjust and control the structure and morphology of the metal compounds and protect the particles from aggregation as well besides of improving their conductivity, so as to obtain higher performance electrode materials.

Alloy-type anodes belong to anode materials with high theoretical capacity, such as Si with a theoretical capacity of 4200 mAh g−1 by forming Li4.4Si alloyed products. [90]However, Alloy-type anode materials face several main challenges, such as slow reaction kinetics and large volume changes, which will result in grim mechanical stress and pulverization of the silicon anode. [123] There are many ways to enhance the electrochemical performance of silicon anodes: one of the most effective strategies is the hybridization of silicon with carbon material to construct the silicon-carbon composite, like surface coating structure in which silicon is coated with amorphous carbon [124] and core-shell structure. [125] Although the addition of amorphous carbon may improve the cycling performance of Si anode, it may hinder the diffusion of Li ions into Si. And as the amount of amorphous carbon increased, the activation process could be prolonged andless saturated Li/Si alloy was formed. [126] Besides, graphene with prominent conductivity, large specific surface area and great mechanical property can become a promising conductive additive and structural support for silicon anode. [127] Nevertheless, classical graphene sheets possess a relatively large size, which will seriously impede the entry of lithium ions into Si, resulting in a low capacity for LIBs. Unlike classical graphene, if GQDs with quantum size are used for the silicon modification, the impact of the coating layer on the lithium ion diffusion between silicon and electrolyte will be greatly reduced. [128]

Kong et al. [128] used citric acid and phenylalanine as raw materials to prepare two amino acid-functionalized GQDs (PF-GQDs, AF-GQDs) that were coated on the surface of silicon nanoparticles (SiNP), and then the products are thermally annealed in Ar/H2 to prepare PF-GQD@SiNP composite materials (Fig. 13 a). The PF-GQDs modification can not only improve the conductivity, but also prevent direct contact between the silicon surface and electrolyte molecules, which effectively improves the coulombic efficiency. The specific capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP electrodes can reach 4066, 3796 and 1820 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1 and 1000 mA g−1, respectively, and the capacity can be maintained at 3068 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 after 100 cycles. They also designed another kind of silicon anode, [129] encapsulating silicon nanoparticles in a three-dimensional interconnection network of multilayer graphene aerogel (MGA-n, inner shell) followed by embedding MGA-n/Si into the bonding layer (outer shell) that consisted of tryptophan acid-functionalized GQDs (Trp-GQD) and sodium alginate (Fig. 13b). The inner shell has higher mechanical strength and electronic conductivity and the decoration of Trp-GQD can vastly improve the mechanical strength, elasticity and conductivity of the outer shell. In addition, the synergistic effect of the inner shell with the outer shell can achieve better properties. The specific capacity of Trp-GQD@MGA-n/Si was 1427 mAh g−1, 1115 mAh g−1 and 637 mAh g−1 at the current density of 100 mA g−1, 1000 mA g−1 and 4200 mA g−1 respectively, and the capacity retention was more than 93.3% at 100 mA g−1 after cycling for 100 cycles with a high coulombic efficiency of 99.8%, showing excellent cycling performance.

In a word, Si anode materials modified with carbon dots can encapsulate silicon atoms inside the carbon dots and prevent the direct contact between silicon surface and electrolyte molecules directly, which greatly improves the coulombic efficiency of pristine Si. In addition, the introduction of GQDs can greatly improve the mechanical strength, elasticity and conductivity of the anode materials.

The high-capacity cathode and anode materials of energy storage are required due to the ever-increasing demands of electric vehicles and power grids, but they are suffering from significant safety issues and poor cycling stability at present. All-solid-state lithium batteries (ASSLBs) have been considered to be particularly promising energy storage systems owing to their superior safety, wide potential window, and long cycling life. [130] Solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) with high ion conductivity are effective strategies to solve the cycling life and safety concerns of LIBs. However, the low ionic conductivity at room temperature hinders the development of SPEs.

Gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) have quasi-solid properties and higher ionic conductivity than SPEs. Chen et al. [131] proved that GQDs can change the functionality of gel polymer electrolytes. They used tunable GQDs to adjust the ion-solvent coordination issues in GPEs effectively, which improved the rate performance and cyclic life of LIBs. GPEs are composed of the polymer backbone and salt of LiPF6 dissolved in a carbonate solvent. The introduction of GQDs into the polymer backbone can inhibit the formation of ion-solvent clusters and promote the migration of lithium ions in gel electrophoresis. GPEs modified with GQDs can reach a capacity of 155 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and capacity retention can reach about 100 % at 5 C after 500 cycles when they are assembled into Li|electronic|LiFePO4 batteries. In conclusion, the introduction of GQDs can minimize ion-solvent clusters and the degree of solvation of Li+ in GPEs, and lithium atoms can be uniformly deposited on the lithium metal electrode, which can prevent the formation of dendrites.

Nanofillers can improve the ionic conductivity of SPEs, and conductivity enhancement is more beneficial to the smaller size of nanofillers. Our group [132] introduced CQDs into the polyethylene oxide (PEO) matrix to form the nanocomposite polymer electrolytes (NPEs). We obtained the PEO/CQDs-NPEs composites by mixing CQDs, PEO, LiClO4 or NaClO4 salts, and studied the effects of CQDs on the ion migration behavior and physical and chemical properties of PEO/CQDs-NPEs. The results show that the highly dispersed CQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups provide many Lewis acid sites, which can effectively promote the dissociation of LiClO4 or NaClO4 and the adsorption of ClO4 −. Furthermore, CQDs can also improve the amorphous nature of the PEO matrix, which makes PEO/CQDs-NPEs possess excellent ion conductivity and high lithium/sodium ion mobility. In addition, PEO/CQDs-NPEs can also promote the uniform deposition and stripping of lithium/sodium, thereby resulting in effective inhibition of the growth of dendrites.
The application of carbon dots in the solid electrolyte is not comprehensive enough and needs extensive exploration in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of CDs in lithium-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the inherent defects of various electrode materials, the modification of CDs is an effective method. GQDs and CQDs with graphite structure can be directly used as electrode materials for LIBs and SIBs that shows satisfactory electrochemical performance. Some of the CDs can also be used as electrode materials for LIBs after high-temperature treatment, showing similar electrochemical performance to carbon-based materials, but the large-scale preparation of CDs is an urgent problem to be solved. Conductive CQDs and GQDs can improve the conductivity and specific surface area of electrode materials and accelerate the transmission of electrons and ions when they are used to modify the surface of carbon-based, titanium-based and metal oxide/sulfide materials. A proper amount of GQDs doping can also be used as a binder to improve the structural stability of MoS2 electrode materials and further improve the cyclic stability of batteries. Moreover, CDs can also induce Mn3O4 particles to form an octahedral structure in the composite materials and this octahedral structure of Mn3O4 can facilitate lower charge transfer resistance and improved coulombic efficiency of LIBs. However, its detailed induction mechanism and the possibility of inducing the morphology of other metal oxides need to be further explored. In addition, CDs also show great application potential in solid polymer electrolytes. Besides of improving the conductivity, they can also promote the uniform deposition of lithium/sodium and effectively inhibit the growth of dendrites.

With the rapid development of various electronic products and electric vehicles, the demands for LIBs are ever-increasing, and the limitation of lithium resources will hinder the continuous development of LIBs. SIBs are considered to be a promising supplement to LIBs because of the rich sodium resources and similar properties to lithium and the electrochemical mechanism of SIBs is similar to that of LIBs. In the charging process, Na+ extracts from the cathode and migrates to the anode to react with anode materials and then Na+ removes from the anode and migrates to the cathode to return to the initial state during the discharging process, which is accompanied by the transfer of electrons in external circuits to provide electrical energy. [133,134] CDs are called "design additives", which can adjust the structure and morphology of electrode materials, and play a vital role in improving the initial coulombic efficiency and electrochemical performance of LIBs and these advantages are also applicable to SIBs.

Similar to lithium-ion batteries, VO2 tailored with CDs can enhance the electrochemical performance of SIBs as well. When GVG synthesised by Chao et al. [92] was used as the cathode of SIBs, the structure of GVG enables SIBs to charge and discharge at a high rate of 120 C and the sodium storage performance of GVG was far better than previously reported electrodes of vanadium oxide. [135,136] Moreover, at the ultrahigh rate of 120 C, the power density of the GVG electrode is as high as 42 kW kg−1 with an energy density of more than 100 Wh kg−1 (Fig. 7e). C-VOCQD fabricated by Balogun et al. [93] also showed excellent electrochemical performance in SIBs, and its energy density and power density for Na storage can reach 264 W kg−1 and 41,333 W kg−1, respectively, which are higher than the reported vanadium oxide-based cathodes for SIBs before (Fig. 7h).

Sodium vanadium fluorophosphate (Na3V2O2x(PO4)2F3–2x(0≤x≤1) as a cathode material for SIBs has excellent performance, including high energy density, high working voltage (3.6–4 V) and small volume changes. An excellent GQDs-coated Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F@C nano-rectangular composite (NVOPF@C) that grown along the optimal orientation was designed and constructed by Deng et al. [137] Coating GQDs on the surface of the NVOPF nanocube can not only prevent the agglomeration of the NVOPF nanocube, but also accelerate the charge transfer and improve the ion conductivity. The discharge capacity of NVOPF@C nanocube can reach 127 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C (1 C = 130 mA g−1), and it can provide a capacity of 70 mAh g−1 at 45 C with a capacity retention of 81% after 2000 cycles and the coulombic efficiency is about 100%, which shows that this NVOPF@C nanocube has great electrochemical reversibility and structural stability even at the high rate for long-term cycling.

Recently, Liu et al. [138] systematically investigated the influence of the solvothermal reaction time and the content of CDs on the structure, morphology and electrochemical performance of CDs modified Na3V2(PO4)2F3 (NVPF@CQD) hierarchical microspheres that were fabricated by a solvothermal method followed by heat treatment. It was found that the morphology and microstructure of the NVPF@CQD composite can be regulated by controlling the amount of CDs and prolonging reaction time will be conducive to the nucleation and growth of the original micro-cuboids and beneficial to the construction of cuboid-assembled primary microspheres. In the solvothermal reaction process, CDs were employed as “designer additives” to induce the morphological evolution of Na3V2(PO4)2F3 from micro-cuboids to cuboid-assembled hierarchical microspheres with a larger size but smaller building blocks, bringing about better ion migration dynamics. Impressively, the NVPF@CQD electrode presented awesome rate performances of 126.6 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C and 84.7 mAh g−1 at 50 C, and achieved excellent cycling stability, when it is used as the cathode in SIBs. Moreover, towards practical applications, a sodium-ion full-cell was constructed with the NVPF@CQD cathode and commercial hard carbon anode and exhibited a high operating voltage and excellent cyclic stability.

The application of carbon dots in cathode materials of SIBs has not been widely studied and the applications of carbon dots in other types of cathode materials and the specific mechanism need to be continuously explored in the future.

Carbon materials have the advantages of abundant resources, great conductivity, low cost, non-toxicity, high safety and their feasibility of storing sodium that has been confirmed. When graphite anode had been confirmed its inferior capacity of 35 mAh g−1 for sodium storage in 1988, [139] suitable carbon-based materials were being improved and explored vigorously to enable effective accommodation of sodium-ions with larger radius (1.02 Å). Although graphite can be used to store sodium through the co-intercalation of Na+ and the solvent into graphite, the intercalation of solvent molecules would induce the increase of resistance and demand of extra electrolyte and the large volume changes from the co-intercalation behavior would lead to the pulverization of graphite particles during cycling, resulting in the degradation of cycling stability. Otherwise, various amorphous carbon materials and biomass-derived carbon materials were employed as anode materials for SIBs and heteroatom-doping was also used to enhance the electrochemical sodium storage performance. [133,140] However, the development of new carbon materials with high initial coulombic efficiency, superior rate capability, long cyclic life and low cost is still urgent from the view of further optimizing the entire performance of batteries.

The previously mentioned CQDs [95] and GQDs [96] show excellent sodium storage performance when directly applied to SIBs. Furthermore, CDs can be transformed into other carbon materials under certain conditions due to the rich surface functional groups and ultrasmall size. Our group [69]found that the 0D CQDs can be evolved into a 3D porous carbon framework (PCFs, Fig. 14 a-d) at high temperatures. which proved that CDs can play an important role in the structural design of carbon materials. The morphology and microstructure of PCFs gained from annealing at 800 °C in Ar atmosphere are characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 14b and c, which exhibit a structure of fluffy and porous 3D framework made up of interconnected and twisty nanosheets, and the interlayer spacing of 0.42 nm can be apparently seen from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 14d, demonstrating that the 3D PCFs are appropriate for the insertion of Na+. The 3D PCFs can behave an extremely long cyclic life and excellent rate performance when they are used for SIBs anodes (Fig. 14e,f). At 0.1 A g−1, the 3D PCFs electrode displays an initial capacity of 356.1 mAh g−1 and keeps at 303.3 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles. And when the current density is added up to 5 A g−1, the initial charging capacity can also reach 104.1 mAh g−1 and can be keeped at 99.8 mAh g−1 after 10,000 cycles.

Inaddition, we [70] also constructed phosphorus-doped carbon nanosheets (P-CNSs) composed of ultrathin sheets with large area by calcining the mixture of CDs (Fig. 14l) and NaH2PO4 under Ar atmosphere, which realized the transformation from zero-dimensional CDs to two-dimensional nanosheets doped with phosphorus (Fig. 14g-k). The insightful transformation from CDs to P-CNSs was explored in detail. It was obviously discovered that the temperature of thermal treatment has a regulatory effect on the morphology and structure of carbon nanosheets and the calcining temperature is also of great importance for the phosphorus content in the obtained P-CNSs. Furthermore, it can be also found that the mass ratio of CDs to NaH2PO4 has a certain influence on the morphology, structure and composition of the final products. When P-CNSs are used as the anode materials of SIBs, they exhibit excellent performance for the electrochemical storage of sodium (Fig. 14m). The P-CNSs electrode delivers a high specific capacity of 328 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, and the capacity can still reach 108 mAh g−1 even at 20 A g−1. And when the current density restores to 0.1 A g−1, the capacity can be restored to 320 mAh g−1, proving excellent rate performance and electrochemical reversibility. Moreover, the p-CNSs also show satisfactory cycling stability, the capacity can still reach 149 mAh g−1 at the current density of 5 A g−1 after 5000 cycles. This work will provide an important reference for the development of carbon materials derived from CDs. It has been certified that the Na-containing compound is an efficient catalyst to boost the reaction potential and weaken the C-O bond to increase the reaction rate and expedite the carbon revolution. Under the condition of high temperature and sodium catalysis, the CDs with abundant oxygenic functional groups can be quickly decomposed to generate rich carbon atoms that then will grow into carbon nanosheets by self-assemble. The gas generating from the fast pyrolysis of CDs can give rise to instantaneous interior high pressure, which can restrain the restack of nanosheets, causing the generation of ultrathin sheets. We [141]introduced melamine in the CQDs self-assembly process and prepared three-dimensional nitrogen-doped porous carbons (NPCMs) as well. Nitrogen doping can improve external defects and electrical conductivity and the NPCMs show superb electrochemical performances when used as anode materials for SIBs (Fig. 14n-r). Later, we also introduced nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus in the self-assemblly process of CDs and prepared sulfur-doped, sulfur/nitrogen-co-doped, phosphorus/sulfur-co-doped carbon materials successfully that show favorable sodium storage performances as well. [142-144]

We also found that the CDs can be used to construct various dimensional carbon materials by thermal treatment of the mixture of CDs and metal chlorides. We [145] made use of the regulatory effect of temperature-induced intermediates (e.g.,ZnO, Ni, and Cu) from low-cost salts (ZnCl2, NiCl2, and CuCl) to realizethe evolution of 0 D CDs (Fig. 15 a-c) to 1D carbon fibers (CNFs, Fig. 15d-f), 2D large-size carbon nanosheets (CNSs, Fig. 15g-i) and 3D porous carbon framework (CFW, Fig. 15j-l). The evolution mechanisms are illustrated as follows (Fig. 15m): (1) the “orient induction” to evoke the “vine style” growth mechanism of ZnO; (2) the “dissolution-precipitation”of Ni; and (3) the “surface adsorption self-limited” of Cu. This evolution induce the formation of different carbon structures with different electrochemical properties, as shown in Fig. 15n. 1D CNFs from 700 °C as the anode of SIBs displays a high Na-storage capacity of 301.2 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 200 cycles and 107 mAh g−1 at 5.0 A g−1 after 5000 cycles. Besides, 2D CNSs and 3D CFW provide a Na-storage capacity of 92.2 and 90.9 mAh g−1 at a large current density of 5.0 A g−1 after 3000 cycles respectively, also showing great rate performance and cyclic performance. Constructing multi-dimensional carbon materials from CDs is helpful to the preparation and wide applications of carbon materials. This elaborate study opens up a new way toward the design of carbon with multidimensions from carbon dots and an in-depth understanding of their sodium-storage specialties.

Hard carbon is considered a promising anode for SIBs due to its best cost-effectiveness. However, compared with the graphite anode of LIBs, its specific capacity and initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) are still lower. Therefore, it is necessary for hard carbon anode materials to improve their specific capacity and ICE. Xie et al. [146] extracted the cellulose carbon dots (CCDs) from the supernatant of the hydrothermal carbon dots as a precursor that directly dried and further carbonized to be used as the anode of SIBs. Compared with solid carbon spheres, they have a lower specific surface area, fewer defects and higher graphite and show a higher sodium storage capacity. CCDs carbonized at 1300°C have an ICE as high as 91%. On the other hand, CCDs exhibit a specific capacity over 300 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1 and provide a high energy density of 248 Wh kg−1 in a full battery composed of NaNi1/3Fe1/3Mn1/3O2 as the cathode. This discovery is also universal to other biomass, making the neglected "waste" in the hydrothermal process have great development potential. This new discovery from a simple traditional method provides a new direction for the design of future commercial high-performance SIBs.

Graphene, as one of the outstanding carbon materials, is also expected to become commercial anode material for SIBs. Liu et al. [147] used the method for self-polymerizing aniline monomer into GO to synthesize a graphene block (N-CDGB) pillared by edge nitrogen-rich carbon dots with ultra-high volume/mass capacity and ultra-long cyclic stability of SIBs (Fig. 15o-q). Due to the large bulk density of 1.5 g cm−3, the pillared graphene structure and the high content of edge nitrogen doping (95%), the N-CDGB exhibits strong structural stability, the fast transfer rate of ion/electron and more active centers for sodium storage. Theoretical calculations show that embedding edge nitrogen-rich carbon dots in graphene sheets can greatly increase the adsorption energy of Na+, which is beneficial to sodium insertion and removal. The N-CDGB electrode exhibits a volume/mass capacity as high as 780 mAh cm−3/520 mAh g−1 at a current density of 0.02 A g−1, which far exceeds that of graphene and hard carbon materials. In addition, the volume/mass capacity of the N-CDGB electrode can be maintained at 118 mAh g−1/177 mAh cm−3 at 10 A g−1 and its structure and capacity can be well maintained after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1, proving that the graphene electrode supported by CDs can provide higher reversible capacity and excellent long-cycle stability.

Different carbon-based materials with favorable electrochemical properties can be derived from carbon dots under different conditions with the addition of different assistant agents, which provides new possibilities for the design of carbon-based materials. Furthermore, carbon-based materials with the modification of CDs can also improve their electrochemical properties, which provides a feasible method for modification of carbon-based materials.

Layered sodium oxide (Na2Ti3O7) with high energy density is considered to be a superior anode material for SIBs due to its low cost, good cycling stability and appropriate voltage platform. Carbon coating has always been considered as an effective means to increase the conductivity and diffusion coefficient of Na+. Kong et al. [148] used Na2Ti3O7 nanofiber arrays modified by N-GQDs (Na2Ti3O7 NFAs) to design a new type of binder-free anode that was grown directly on flexible carbon textiles (CTs). The three-dimensional layered nano-arrays constructed by ultra-thin Na2Ti3O7 nanosheets can provide a large specific surface area and a short diffusion channel for ions and electrons. Moreover, the protection of N-GQDs greatly improves the conductivity and stability of the nanofiber array structure and helps to accelerate the diffusion kinetics of Na+. When 3D multilayer Na2Ti3O7@N-GQDs/CTs are used as the binder-free anode of SIBs, they can provide a specific capacity of 158 mAh g−1 after 30 cycles at 4 C (1 C = 177 mA g−1) and the capacity retention is about 92.5% after 1000 cycles, showing excellent cyclic performance. In addition, Na2Ti3O7@N-GQDs/CTs can also be assembled with Na3V2(PO4)3@NC/CTs cathode to form a flexible full battery, which can also exhibit high flexibility, excellent long-cycle stability and extremely high energy and power density.

TiO2 is also one of the potential anode materials for SIBs and carbon materials are widely introduced as conductive media in order to improve the conductivity of TiO2. Studies have shown that adding some carbon additives into the synthesis system of the metal oxide can not only increase the electrical conductivity of electrodes, but also adjust their morphology and structure to optimize their electrochemical performance. Our group [149] proposed CDs as a "designer additives" for graphene-rich petal-like rutile TiO2composite (G/P-RTiO2). As effective functional inducers, CDs can tailor the morphology of TiO2 from nanoparticles to nanoneedles, and then they will further self-organize into a three-dimensional petal-like structure (Fig. 16 a,b,d), which can reduce the diffusion pathways of Na ions and improve the electrochemical storage performance, as shown in Fig. 16c. When G/P-RTiO2 is applied as the anode in SIBs, it delivers a high capacity of 245.3 mAh g−1and an initial coulombic efficiency of about 50.6% at 0.25 C (1 C = 335 mA g−1). Strikingly, it can maintain a capacity of 182.6 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C after cycling for 1000 cycles and the capacity retention can reach 94.4% at 10 C after 4000 cycles. This study demonstrates that the unique CD-tailored unique nanostructure, accompanied by graphene-rich encapsulation, can be a significant method to improve the electrochemical energy-storage systems for metal/metal oxide materials.

Our group [150] also designed a hierarchical composite of anatase TiO2 uniformly modified by carbon, exploiting CQDs as homogeneous carbon additives and functional inducers for structure tailoring and surface modification (Fig. 16e-g). The spherical pinecone-like structure is constructed by mesoporous nanosized branches consisting of ultrafine nanograins with diameters below 10 nm, which are homogeneously tailored by CQDs through Ti-C chemical bonds. This unique structure significantly boosts the electronic conductivity of TiO2 through not only the conductive carbon but also the accompanied oxygen deficiencies. Notably, kinetics analysis of CTO electrode indicated an improved sodium ions intercalation pseudocapacitive behavior in an extended rate range, which is of great benefit to a fast sodium storage process with a long cycling life. When this unique carbon-coupled TiO2 composite (CTO) was utilized for sodium storage, it provided a high reversible specific capacity of 264.1 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C (33.6 mA g−1) and the capacity still remained 108.2 mAh g−1 at 10 C after 2000 cycles with a capacity retention of 94.7% (Fig. 16h). Recently, Wu et al. [151] synthesized a new mesoporous architecture of nitrogen-doped anatase TiO2 by adding CQDs solution in the hydrothermal process (CQDs@TiO2, Fig. 16i-k). The CQDs@TiO2 material had several characteristics of good crystallinity, high conductivity, large surface area and interconnected pore structure, thus exhibiting superior sodium storage capacity after annealing at 500 °C.

In conclusion, these studies further prove that carbon dots play a significant part in controlling the morphology of metal oxides. It is hoped that more types of carbon-modified metal oxides for SIBs will be developed in the future.

Metal anodes (eg. Sn, Ge, Sb) have been proposed for SIBs due to their high mass/volume capacity. Antimony (Sb) has a high theoretical capacity of 660 mAh g−1, a suitable redox potential range and high conductivity. However, significant volume expansion up to 390% and slow kinetics during the sodiumization/de-sodiumization process lead to poor cycling stability. In order to solve these problems, researchers are committed to using carbon or other conductive substrates to support and disperse active nanoparticles to prepare composite structures. Liu et al.
