[152] have synthesized Sb@CQDs complex successfully at room temperature, using CQDs and sodium borohydride as the dispersant and reducing agent, respectively. As functional inducers, CQDs can regulate the nucleation and growth of Sb particles during the reduction process and prevent their aggregation. With the dose of CQDs increasing, the size of Sb@CQDs can be further reduced, which further proves that the size of Sb particles can be controlled by adjusting the amount of CQDs. The prepared Sb@CQDs composite with about 7 nm can buffer the volume changes and shorten the diffusion distance of sodium ions. Besides, the residual CQDs in the composite can also be used as electronic conductive channels to improve electronic conductivity. Thanks to the modification of CQDs, Sb@CQDs composite can provide a high specific capacity of 635 mAh g−1 and 334 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 2 A g−1, respectively. When the current density returns to 0.1 A g−1, the reversible capacity can be restored to 633 mAh g−1, proving the great rate performance of Sb@CQDs composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of CDs in sodium-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to LIBs, electrode materials modified with CDs are a new strategy to comprehensively improve the electrochemical performance of SIBs. CDs can form a porous carbon framework by the high-temperature treatment which provides great inspirations for the design of new carbon materials and the storage performance of sodium. In other aspects, CDs can be used as conductive materials to modify the surface of electrode materials to accelerate the transfer of ions and electrons and can provide larger specific surface area and abundant active sites to improve the electrochemical performance of SIBs. In addition, CDs can tailor the morphology of TiO2 from nanoparticles into nano-needles when they are used as functional inducers, generally applicable to a variety of metal oxide materials. Moreover, CDs can also adjust the nucleation and growth of Sb particles during the reduction process and prevent their aggregation.

Potassium ion batteries (PIBs) have gradually become another promising supplementary device for LIBs due to their abundant potassium resources and their working principles are similar to LIBs. Whereas, electrode materials for PIBs are still in the early stages of exploration, and carbon materials are considered to be ideal anode materials due to their stable performance and abundant sources. Graphite is a commercial anode for LIBs, its cyclic performance and rate performance are also very poor even at extremely low current densities because of the large radius of K+ (1.38 Å) and the limited spacing between graphitic layers, accompanied by slow dynamics and huge volume expansion. Different from the long-range ordered and dense structure of graphitic carbon, amorphous carbon is composed of disordered graphite and graphene layers with enlarged interlayer spacing and abundant nanopores, which can promote the adsorption and insertion/extraction of potassium ions cooperatively. [133,153]

Hard carbon has a more disordered nanostructure, which can create more space for buffering drastic volume changes. Our group [154] prepared a kind of honeycomb hard carbon (HHC) with uniform pore structure and ordered layered structure successfully, using CQDs as raw materials, as shown in Fig. 17 a-d. As the anode materials of PIBs, HHC can provide a high specific capacity of 195.3 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, and possess capacity retention of 67.43% after 150 cycles (Fig. 17e,f). To further explore the potassium storage performance of carbon materials and develop high-performance anodes of PIBs, our group [155] designed hollow nanostructured N-doped carbon materials (p-HNCs, Fig. 17g-j) with the modification of CQDs for the first time, inspired by the heteroatom doping and porous hollow structure of LIBs and SIBs. The formation of suitable CQDs micelles is the key to the preparation of hollow nanocarbons and CQDs micelles can act as templates and pore formers. The hollow interior and small channels formed by the pyrolysis of CQDs are conducive to the penetration of electrolyte and the diffusion of K+, which can improve the electrochemical performance of PIBs consequently (Fig. 17k-m).The prepared p-HNCs can provide a specific capacity of 254 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles and 160 mAh g−1 at a high current density of 1.0 A g−1 after 800 cycles when they are applied to be anodes of PIBs. This work proves that CQDs have a special role in structural construction and provides a basis for the design of high-performance carbon anodes of PIBs.

Recently, Zhang et al. [156] constructed a high-performance anode of PIBs with a flexible 3D hybrid structure by introducing CDs on the surface of rGO (CDs@rGO, Fig. 17n-r). The CDs@rGO has efficient transfer channels of electrons and ions due to its unique structure, which can improve the reaction kinetics. In addition to modifying the surface of rGO, CDs can also be used as a binder to ensure the stability of the electrode structure and reduce the effect of binder on batteries. Moreover, the introduction of CDs with oxygen-containing functional groups is of great benefit to orderly induce K+ to form a stable SEI film on the surface of the electrode. The independent and flexible anode can deliver a high capacity of 310 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and ultra-long cycling life of 244 mAh g−1 at 200 mA g−1 after 840 cycles and excellent rate performance of 221 mAh g−1 at 500 mA g−1. Even after six consecutive current changes, the specific capacity of 185 mAh g−1 can be provided at 500 mA g−1, which is better than many existing carbon-containing anodes of PIBs. It is found that the introduction of CDs has a great effect on the surface structure and chemical bonds of rGO and leads to changes in electrochemical properties. The results of electrochemical tests combined with cyclic voltammetry and in situ XRD showed that the introduction of CDs makes the potassium storage mechanism of rGO change from potassium/depotassium to the combination of potassium/depotassium and adsorption/desorption, which improves the specific capacity, cyclic performance and rate performance significantly, making it possible for rGO to store potassium rapidly and reversibly. The strategy of introducing CDs to enhance the electrochemical performance of carbon-based materials provides a new idea and vision for the design of electrode materials for PIBs.

Researches on electrode materials for PIBs are still in their infancy, and traditional CDs coatings or composite electrode materials have not been extensively studied. However, CDs are a key factor in the preparation of various nano-carbons as anode materials. The honey-comb hard carbon material designed from CDs has ideal potassium storage performances and the interior and small channels of the hollow N-doped carbon materials formed by the pyrolysis of CDs are conducive to the penetration of electrolyte and diffusion of K+. Furthermore, CDs can also be used as a binder to modify the surface of GO to ensure the stability of the electrode structure. In a word, the design and modification of CDs in carbon materials provide a forward-looking guide for the design of electrode materials for PIBs.

Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries directly use lithium and sulfur as anode and cathode, and they can produce a theoretical capacity of 1675 mAh g−1 through the complete reduction of S8 to form Li2S. Their theoretical energy density is as high as 2567 Wh kg−1 and the actual energy density is also as high as 500 Wh kg−1, which is 2–3 times larger than that of current commercial LIBs and they are considered a candidate system for the next generation of energy storage systems. [157,158] The practical application of Li-S batteries is hindered by four major challenges, which are the four major problems that must be solved in the process of moving towards practical applications. [159]

The first challenge comes from the insulating properties of sulfur and various intermediate polysulfides formed during the discharging process, which will lead to excessively high internal resistance and limit the utilization of active materials. The increase in internal resistance will also cause greater polarization, thereby reducing the energy efficiency of the battery. Furthermore, with the structure and morphology of lithium polysulfide (Li2Sx) formed during the cycling process changing, the electrochemical contact with the cathode of sulfur becomes unstable. The second challenge is about the electrochemical process of lithium-sulfur batteries. The polysulfides (mainly Li2S4 - Li2S8) produced during the cycling process are easy to react with the organic solvent or dissolve in the electrolyte, resulting in an irreversible decline in the capacity of the cathode. Moreover, the dissolved polysulfides can migrate to the anode through the so-called “shuttle effect”, producing an inactive layer mainly composed of Li2S2 and Li2S, which can further reduce the efficiency of the battery. The third challenge is related to volume changes of sulfur during cycling. The volume of sulfur increases by 79% after discharging and large volume expansion can cause Li2S to lose contact with the conductive matrix or current collector, resulting in rapid capacity degradation. The fourth challenge is about the electrode of lithium metal and it is affected by the formation of the solid electrolyte interface and the growth of lithium dendrites. Lithium dendrites can grow on the separator and will cause the short circuit, which is likely to pose a risk of inflammability. Researchers have made great efforts to design and improve sulfur cathodes, electrolytes and separators to overcome these challenges, so that lithium-sulfur batteries with high energy density, high rate performance, and long life can be put into practical use as soon as possible.

Based on the previous exploration of LIBs and SIBs, GQDs have been reported as composite components and coating materials for energy storage devices. GQDs were introduced into Li-S batteries by Park et al. [32] for the first time. They prepared GQDs-S and GQDs-S/carbon black (CB) as electrodes through simple hybridization and hydrothermal processes. GQDs are uniformly coated on sulfur particles and they can be tightly combined with CB to form a dense GQDs-S/CB composite structure, as shown in Fig. 18 a-e. Fig. 18f-1 is a schematic diagram depicting a traditional Li-S battery, in which the anode and cathode are lithium metal and S/CB composite, respectively. In Fig. 18f-2, GQDs that contain hydrophobic aromatic regions and hydrophilic defect regions, are introduced into the S/CB cathode and interact with CBs and sulfur, respectively. The SEM images in Fig. 18g,h confirmed that the GQDs-S/CB composite electrode is densely filled with CBs, while the surface of the sulfur particles in the S/CB electrode is partially exposed. It is proved that nano-scale GQDs with rich oxygenic functional groups can strengthen the structural integrity of traditional micro-scale sulfur-carbon composite electrodes and a dense layered structure can be formed, which can promote the conduction of charge and reduce the irreversible loss of high-order polysulfides (HOPSs). As shown in Fig. 18j and k, GQDs-S/CB electrode exhibited excellent rate performance and cyclic performance compared with S/CB due to the introduction of GQDs. In addition, the author further clarified that the oxygenic functional groups of GQDs can help prevent the dissolution of HOPSs through the formation of C-S bonds (sulfurophilic properties) by experimental observations and theoretical calculations, providing an important breakthrough for the introduction of GQDs into Li-S batteries and enabling them to become high-performance and low-cost batteries for advanced energy storage systems.

In order to meet the demands of the commercialization of Li-S batteries, a high load of sulfur and woking at high current density have become key factors that restrict the commercialization of Li-S batteries. Hu et al. [160] designed and prepared polyethyleneimine functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDots) to improve the performance of high-sulfur-loaded Li-S batteries and the working performance at high current densities. PEI-CDots are integrated with acetylene black (AB), sulfur and polyvinylidene fluoride to form the PEI-CDots@AB/S cathode (Fig. 18l). As shown in Fig. 18q, where the PEI-CDots@AB/S cathode showed more satisfactory rate performance especially at higher current densities, which proved that the introduction of PEI-CDots into the AB/S cathode can significantly improve the capacity under the condition of high sulfur loading and high current densities. It is quite a challenge for Li-S batteries with high sulfur loads to work in high current densities (>5 mA cm−2) for long cycles. The Li-S batteries modified with PEI-CDots exhibited a large area capacity of 3.3 mAh cm−1 under the condition of high sulfur loading of 6.6 mg and a large current density of 8 mA cm−2, and the capacity decline was only 0.07 % per cycle after cycling for 400 cycles (Fig. 18r). The mechanism of performance improvement under high current density and high sulfur loading was also studied through detailed EIS and CV research combined with DFT calculation (Fig. 18m-o). The results show that PEI-CDots can improve the conductivity of Li+ near the solid-electrolyte interface and the amine groups of the PEI-CDots can provide multiple adsorption sites, which can effectively inhibit the dissolution of polysulfides. This study proves that the CDs modified sulfur cathode system has great potential in practical applications of Li-S batteries.

Exploring an economical preparation route for electrode materials is also the key to the commercialization of Li-S batteries. Chabu et al. [157] simply prepared a kind of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide composite (N,S-CDs/rGO) by hydrothermally and annealing the mixture of graphene oxide and albumen. The composite has a porous structure and the N, S-CDs/rGO@S cathode exhibits better rate performance and higher utilization of sulfur. The N,S-CDs/rGO@S65 (sulfur content is 65wt%) cathode can deliver a capacity of 1550 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and a capacity of 740.8 mAh g−1 even at a large current density of 7 C, and the capacity can be maintained at 427.9 mAh g−1 at 5 C after 600 cycles. This work opens up a new perspective for the design of sulfur cathodes modified by low-cost co-doped CDs.

As for Li-S batteries, embedding sulfur in supporting materials has been shown to inhibit the “shuttle effect” of polysulfide significantly and improve cyclic performance, but it always requires expensive and laborious manufacturing processes to prepare these composites. So that various easy and cost-effective methods have been explored, including the functionalization of separators with carbon materials. [161,162]

CQDs can be considered as an effective adsorbent for chemically bonding polysulfide in Li-S batteries because of their large specific surface area and rich oxygenic functional groups. But they must be combined with conductive materials, because CQDs prepared by some methods are non-conductive. Pang et al. [163] designed a new separator coated with ultra-light and multi-wall carbon nanotubes/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (MWCNT/NCQD), and then this separator was applied to Li-S batteries (Fig. 19 a-d). The coating of MWCNTs/NCQDs will act as physical shielding and chemisorption to prevent the shuttle of polysulfide, and the synergistic effect of MWCNTs and NCQDs can enable Li-S batteries to deliver a relatively high initial discharge capacity of 1330.8 mAh g−1 and great cyclic performance (Fig. 19e,f). Compared with the separator modified with MWCNT-s, the separator modified with MWCNT-s/NCQDs can improve the capacity retention ability and self-discharge suppression ability of Li-S batteries. This study proves that the design of MWCNT-s/NCQDs-coated separator is also a feasible way to develop commercial Li-S batteries successfully.

The electrochemical performance of lithium metal batteries will deteriorate gradually due to the growth of lithium dendrites, which seriously affects their safety and hinders their commercialization. The formation of dendrites is mainly due to the uneven distribution of lithium-ions flow and electric field on the interface between electrode and electrolyte. [164,165] Besides, the growth of dendrites can provide a large active surface area for the reaction between lithium metal and electrolyte, and a solid electrolyte (SEI) film will be formed, which will consume active materials and electrolyte irreversibly. [166] More seriously, lithium dendrites can even pierce the separator and come into direct contact with the cathode, causing a short circuit of batteries. Therefore, how to inhibit the growth of lithium dendrites and realize dendrites-free lithium anodes are very important for the practical application of lithium metal batteries. [167]

Hong et al. [168] designed a new electrolyte to achieve uniform deposition of lithium metal, using CNDs as electrolyte additives (Fig. 20 b-e). The synergistic effect of negative charge on the surface and functional groups of CNDs plays a key role in building a strong affinity for Li+. This electrolyte with CNDs can provide a nucleation location for lithium ions and contribute to the uniform deposition of lithium, which can improve the stability of the batteries. As shown in Fig. 20a, CNDs induced lithium deposition and dissection effectively: In the initial stage of electrochemical measurement, the negative charge and various carbon and nitrogen structures in the surface of CNDs can attract Li+ together through electrostatic force, which improved the shuttle ability of Li+ and then CDs-Li+ clusters were formed. The CDs-Li+ clusters moved towards the current collector under the electric field, and they can distribute uniformly on the current collector affected by mutual repulsive forces. In addition, they also demonstrated that the functional U-CDs can work well in all semi-cells, symmetric cells and full-cells. The Coulombic efficiency reaches 95.5% in semi-cells of Li/Cu after 100 cycles, the overpotential is stable after 500 cycles in symmetric cells of Li/Li and the capacity retention reaches 99.9% after 100 cycles in full cells of Li/LiCoO2. In general, Li deposition behavior assisted with CDs can reduce the risk of growth of Li dendrites, so as to advance the cyclic stability of Li-S batteries. On the other hand, the practical application of CDs in the system of other types of lithium metal batteries also has great potential because of their synthetic feasibility, excellent dispersibility in the traditional electrolyte and great compatibility while working in batteries.

Hu et al. [169] reported the method to inhibit the growth of lithium dendrites with the additives of GQDs into the electrolyte in Li-S batteries as well, and they realized uniform deposition of lithium metal and the dendrite-free anode of Li-S batteries with large area capacity. SEM image was used to study the morphology of Li coating on Cu foil, and the effect and mechanism of GQDs on the nucleation and growth of lithium were clarified. As shown in Fig. 20f, It is clear that lithium metal realizes the nucleation form of dense and uniform dendrite-free columnar structure in the electrolyte with GQDs, while the non-uniform nucleation form is displayed in the electrolyte without GQDs. As the initial nucleation sites of lithium, GQDs can induce a dense and uniform structure of lithium deposition by adjusting the electric field and ion flow near the interface between electrode and electrolyte.

Encapsulation strategies are broadly used to alleviate the dissolution and diffusion of polysulfide (LiPS), but they always experience irreversible structural failure arising from the repetitive drastic volume changes during cycling. Inspired by the process of endovascular thrombosis and thrombolysis, Fu et al. [170] designed nitrogen-doped carbon dots as additives in the electrolyte of Li-S batteries with similar function of platelet, so as to suppress the shuttle and dissolution of LiPS by constructing a polysulfide protective layer that can be restored to reduce the loss of sulfur. As shown in Fig. 20h, the LiPS/N-CDs solidification layer is generated immediately after adding Li2S6 solution and 0.3 wt % N-CDs into the electrolyte, and there is almost no color or volume changes after 30 min, indicating that the solidification layer can effectively prevent Li2S6 from diffusing into the electrolyte. On the contrary, it is very obvious that Li2S6 diffuses rapidly in the electrolyte without N-CDs. As it is shown in Fig. 20i, the electrolyte without N-CDs turns to red-brown after discharging for 2 h with the concentration of long-chain LiPS in batteries increasing, while the color of electrolyte with 0.3 wt% N-CDs after discharging for 2 h was almost the same as that at the beginning. Moreover, the cathode of sulfur was covered with a thin condensation layer of LiPS/N-CDs after discharging for 1 h, and the condensation layer becomes thicker with the time increasing, which further confirmed the significant condensation effect of N-CDs on LiPS. Based on the experimental results and theoretical analysis, they proposed a reasonable LiPS clotting mechanism (Fig. 20g, j): Firstly, the LiPS derived from the process of discharge interact with the N-CDs in the electrolyte to form a LiPS/N-CD clotting layer on the surface of the sulfur cathode, which prevented the succedent diffusion and dissolution of LiPS and thus solidified the sulfur cathode. Although the extension of the sulfur cathode may destruct the initial clotting layer during discharge, resulting in the diffusion of LiPS, the highly dispersed N-CDs can catch the escaped LiPS and heal the gap. In the discharge process, the LiPS in the LiPS/N-CDs clotting layer were reduced to Li2S. With the N-CDs acting as nucleation sites, the as-generated Li2S grows into hierarchical porous flower-like Li2S crystals, causing the transient disappearance of the clotting layers. In the charging process, the formation of the protective layer is entirely reversible. As shown in Fig. 20k and l, the cathode of sulfur can provide a high reversible capacity of 891 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and it still maintained 589 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles, and its cycling stability can be up to 1000 cycles at 2 C. The solidification performance for polysulfide of N-CDs was due to their abundant N atoms and oxygenic groups on the surface that can enhance the interaction between N-CDs and LiPS, in order to realize the high utilization rate of sulfur and long-cycle stability of Li-S batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of CDs in lithium-sulfur batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Although Li-S batteries are considered as potential energy storage devices due to their significant theoretical energy density, there are still several big problems. Not only Sulfur cathodes and separators modified by CDs can improve the conductivity of Li+, but also the functionalized CDs can help to provide abundant adsorption sites to inhibit the shuttle and dissolution of polysulfides effectively and improve the utilization of sulfur. As electrolyte additives, CDs can become nucleation sites for lithium and induce more uniform lithium deposition behavior to improve the cycling stability of Li-S batteries. In general, CDs provide a new method for the design of sulfur cathode, improving the utilization of sulfur and inducing uniform lithium deposition, thereby improving the electrochemical performance of Li-S batteries.

Metal-air batteries are also candidates for lithium-ion battery systems and a typical metal-air battery consists of the metal anode, separator immersed in the ion-conductive electrolyte and the air cathode. In the discharging process, the metal anode is oxidized to form metal ions and release electrons to the external circuit, while on the air cathode, O2 in the air diffuses through the electrode of porous carbon and accepts the electrons generated by the anode, and is reduced to oxygen-containing compounds in the presence of catalysts. The charging process is reversed: metal is reduced at the anode and oxygen is released from the cathode. [171] Generally, metal-air batteries can be divided into two types according to the electrolyte: one is an aqueous electrolyte system, such as zinc-air batteries and the other is the organic electrolyte system, such as lithium-oxygen batteries. Metal-air batteries are deemed to be a more efficient and practical sustainable power supply device due to the characteristics of low cost and environmental friendliness. Nevertheless, there are also some disadvantages like the slow kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction/oxygen evolution reaction (ORR/OER) on the cathode during cycling.

Li-O2 batteries have caught much attention on account of their extremely high energy density and oxygen cathode is generally considered to be the main factor for affecting the electrochemical performance. Therefore, researchers are committed to the study of catalytic active materials for ORR and OER and the mechanism of reaction on the cathode, and the design of electrode structure for high-efficiency charge transportation in order to develop lithium-oxygen batteries with small polarization, high discharging capacity, high cyclic efficiency, great rate performance and high output power. A new method to improve the catalytic activity of CoO through the integration of CDs and oxygen vacancies is proposed by Gao et al. [172] (Fig. 21 a, b). They successfully prepared a CoO with carbon dots and defect oxygen vacancies (CoO/C) by simply calcining the precursor prepared by adjusting Co(Ac)2•4H2O with ethanol. Compared with commercial CoO or pure CoO with oxygen vacancies, the initial capacity, cyclic stability and rate performance of the as-synthesized CoO/C cathode are greatly improved and the overpotential is also reduced, which can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of CDs and oxygen vacancies on ORR and OER. CDs can not only provide a higher activity for ORR, but also improve the conductivity of CoO and stabilize oxygen vacancies in the process of ORR/OER. And oxygen vacancies can strengthen the mobility of electrons and Li+, and combine with O2 and Li2O2 to improve the electrochemical performance of CoO/C synergistically. The initial specific capacity of the CoO/C-based cathode can reach 7011 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1, showing excellent electrocatalytic activity. In addition, the CoO/C-based cathode can still provide a capacity of 4074 mAh g−1 even at a higher current density of 400 mA g−1, and the capacity can still maintain 1000 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles and the charge-discharge curve remains stable.

Li-CO2 batteries can provide an energy density of ∼3500 Wh kg−1 that is 10 times higher than LIBs, based on the redox reaction between lithium anode and carbon dioxide cathode (4 Li + 3 CO2 = 2 Li2CO3 + C). [173] But Li2CO3 formed in the discharging process is hard to decompose, resulting in a large charge overpotential and poor cyclic performance. Hence, there is an urgent need for high-performance and low-cost catalysts to be combined with the electrode structure to develop Li-CO2 batteries with low overpotential and long-cycle capability. Jin et al. [174] proved that carbon quantum dots supported by porous graphene (CQD/hG) is a high-performance metal-free composite catalyst for Li-CO2 batteries. It can promote the rapid generation and decomposition of Li2CO3 in the organic electrolyte and achieve a low overpotential of 1.02 V (Li/Li+) at 0.1 A g−1. The discharge capacity of the Li-CO2 battery is 12,300 mAh g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, and it can be recycled for 235 cycles at the condition of the current density of 1 A g−1 and the cut-off capacity of 500 mAh g−1, showing favorable long-cycle stability.

Zinc-air batteries (ZABs) have drawn much attention for their high energy density, environmental friendliness and good safety, and air cathode is one of the most expensive components and is a key factor in determining the performance of ZABs. The effectiveness of oxygen in the air cathode depends on its degree of dissolution and diffusion in the electrolyte and the electrocatalysis occurs at the three-phase interface of the catalyst, electrolyte and O2. It is significant to maximize the performance of the air cathode by designing the catalyst structure rationally. The formation of defects in the carbon matrix is one of the important ways to improve the catalytic activity. Wang et al. [175] designed a defect-rich graphene/carbon dots composite (N-GH-GQD-1000) by combining the modification of defect-rich GQDs and N-doping, and used it as a new electrocatalyst for ZABs. The TEM image of N-GH-GQD-1000 is shown in Fig. 21c, the presence of black dots in N-GH-GQD-1000 confirmed the existence of GQDs. And GQDs rich in defects can provide more active centers for ORR, so that the composite will provide a great electrocatalytic activity for ORR. It is very obvious that N-GH-GQD-1000 showed better ORR activity than benchmark Pt/C as shown in Fig. 21d. Moreover, water-based ZABs were prepared using this material as the catalyst of the cathode in order to further evaluate the application potential of N-GH-GQD-1000 material. Compared with Pt/C, the N-GH-GQD-1000 cathode exhibited a higher discharge voltage in the current density range of 20–100 mA cm−2 (Fig. 21e), making the power density of ZABs higher. In Fig. 21f, it is shown that the stability of N-GH-GQD-1000 during discharge was better than Pt/C, which proves that N-GH-GQD-1000 can be a substitute for the precious metal-based catalyst of ZABs.

Theoretical calculations show that Co9S8 has favorable catalytic activity in acidic solutions, and Zhang et al. [176] first tried to use CDs to improve the catalytic activity of Co9S8 for ORR and OER. The Co9S8/nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon composite (Co9S8/NSC, Fig. 21g) was successfully prepared with the assistance of CDs, and the introduction of CDs can reduce the growth and aggregation of Co9S8 nanoparticles during the synthesis process and promote their distribution in the carbon matrix, which can ultimately increase the specific surface area and conductivity of the products. The energy gap (ΔE) of the Co9S8/CDs@NSC composite catalyst between the ORR half-wave potential (0.84 V/RHE) and the OER potential (1.62 V/RHE) is 0.78 V at 10 mA cm−2, showing outstanding bifunctional catalytic activity for ORR and OER (Fig. 21h). In addition, the Co9S8/CDs@NSC catalyst exhibited a higher power density than the mixed catalyst of Pt/C+RuO2 and long-cycle stability of 125 h at 10 mA cm−2 when it was utilized as cathode material in ZABs (Fig. 21i, j). Therefore, this study provided a new reference for the design of non-noble metal-based dual-function catalysts for ORR and OER in ZABs. A new 3 D NiCo2S4 nanoarray modified with N-GQDs (N-GQDs/NiCo2S4/CC) was prepared by Liu et al. [177] through simple chemical vulcanization and electrophoretic deposition processes (Fig. 21k). The flexible ZABs based on the air cathode of 3 D N-GQDs/NiCo2S4/CC possess many advantages of high peak power density, great stability, excellent durability and outstanding flexibility. DFT results show that the coupling and synergy between N-GQDs and NiCo2S4 make it have excellent ORR and OER performance, and N-GQDs can provide more active centers that facilitate the adsorption/desorption of O* and OOH*, thereby reducing the overpotential of the electrochemical reaction and improving the performance of ORR and OER.

Carbon-based dual-function electrocatalysts for ORR and OER are potentially cost-effective in replacing precious metal catalysts of energy devices such as fuel cells, metal-air batteries, and photoelectrochemical converters, but the enrichment of active centers is the key to improving their efficiency. Shin et al. [178] modified appropriate CDs on the graphene substrate to provide abundant active sites by selecting appropriate heteroatoms. In order to clarify the compatibility between CDs and substrates and the influence of different functional groups on CDs, a combination of different heteroatoms (N and N-S) doped CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. After the optimization of heat treatment, their electrocatalytic activity for ORR and OER are quite high under certain catalyst loading. Especially the N, S co-doped CDs sample of N,S-CD@gf_a900 shows the best electrocatalytic performance result from the synergistic effect of CDs and doping. Furthermore, Tam et al. [179] simply synthesized graphene hydrogel modified by B-GQDs (GH-BGQD), which had a unique three-dimensional structure, high porosity, and large specific surface area. It can provide sufficient catalytic active centers of B-GQD, which enhances the mass transfer of electrolyte and diffusion of ion. In consequence, the prepared GH-BGQD composite exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity in oxygen reduction reaction, oxygen evolution reaction and hydrogen evolution reaction, and their stability and durability were better than commercial Pt/C and Ir/C catalysts.

As a part of the efficient air electrode, quantum-size CDs with a large number of edge defects can supply more catalytic centers to improve the performance of ORR and OER, which will provide a new solution for the design of non-metallic catalysts. However, the surface groups or defects of CDs are mainly composed of certain non-metallic elements such as oxygen and nitrogen, and transition metal elements may have great potential for doping. Therefore, it is a magnetic choice to carry out studies on a new generation of doped CDs as catalysts for metal-air batteries.

Flow batteries, especially vanadium-based flow batteries (VFBs), have the advantages of large capacity, long cyclic life and low maintenance costs and they are considered the most promising large-scale energy storage devices. [180] The performance of VFBs is highly dependent on their electrodes, separators and electrolytes. Among them, the low electrochemical activity of the electrode has become the most critical issue in flow batteries. Generally speaking, carbon-based materials are promising electrode materials in VFBs due to their high specific surface area and favorable conductivity. [181] Inspired by CDs with high electrocatalytic activity and stable structure, Zhou et al. [182] first prepared a new type of graphite electrode modified by CDs (CDs/GF) for VFBs through the solvothermal method. This new electrode structure has the following advantages: (1) improving the wettability of electrolyte; (2) reducing the charge transfer resistance and promoting the rate performance and stability of VFBs; (3) improving the utilization of electrolyte and power density of VFBs; (4) increasing working efficiency of VFBs under various climatic conditions. CDs/GF electrodes can enhance the performance of VFBs mainly because of the modification of CDs: (1) CDs have a large specific surface area and rich surface edges/defects, which are beneficial to improve catalytic activity; (2) CDs have great water solubility, which is beneficial to the contact between electrodes and electrolyte; (3) CDs have a stable structure, which can ensure the stability of VFBs in long-term cycles.

Researches on CDs in flow batteries is still in its infancy. For example, in addition to carbon-based materials, metal oxide electrodes with high catalytic activity are also excellent choices for flow batteries. However, these materials are expensive and prone to passivation or corrosion. Hence, using CDs to solve the above problems is also worthy of deep studies.

In recent years, alkaline water-based batteries have received more and more attention as a new type of energy storage system and they exhibit higher energy density than supercapacitors and higher power density and longer cycling stability than lithium-ion batteries due to the use of water-based electrolyte and battery-type electrodes. Therefore, they are considered a new type of energy storage system that has great development prospects. [183,184] Zhu et al. [185] reported a new application of 3D-CQD-based composite aerogel as the electrode for alkaline water-based batteries for the first time. They prepared graphite-type CQDs with a yield of more than 40% and then used the CQDs to prepare nitrogen-doped CQDs/reduced graphene oxide/porous Fe2O3 composite aerogels (N-CQDs/rGO/Fe2O3) with different Fe2O3 content. CQDs can be utilized as conductive agents to increase the conductivity of composite aerogels and improve their rate performance. In addition, CQDs can also be used as intercalating agents to prevent rGO from agglomerating, effectively increasing the specific surface area and utilization rate of composite aerogels and increasing the specific capacity. They also prepared large-scale CQDs that were further used as structure-directing agents and conductive media to synthesize a new type of sea urchin-like microsphere composite (N,S-CQDs/NiCo2S4). [186]N, S-CQDs are beneficial to fast electron transmission and the unique micro/nano-structured NiCo2S4 microspheres have Faraday redox characteristics, which can provide large capacity contributions and improve the electrochemical performance. The electrochemical performance of NiCo2S4 and N, S-CQDs/NiCo2S4 electrodes under the three-electrode structure was studied, using 2 M KOH solution as the electrolyte. Compared with the pure NiCo2S4 electrode, the N,S-CQDs/NiCo2S4 composite electrode exhibited a higher specific capacity. Furthermore, the new N, S-CQDs/NiCo2S4//N-rGO/Fe2O3 alkaline water-based battery assembled with Fe2O3 anode represents a high specific energy of 50.2 Wh kg−1, ultra-high power of 9.7 kW kg−1 and excellent cyclic performance with the capacity retention of 91.5% after 5000 cycles at 3 A g−1. This study provides a estimable reference for the design and development of energy storage devices that simultaneously use battery/capacitor composite materials as cathode and anode in aqueous solutions.

With the development of alkaline water-based batteries, research on their electrode materials will become more and more detailed. As good conductive agents and structure-directing agents, CDs can provide a certain reference for the design of electrode materials for alkaline water-based batteries to improve their electrochemical performance.

CDs show great potential in the design of electrode materials, modification of separators and electrolyte additives of different battery systems due to their unique quantum size, great conductivity, abundant functional groups and easy functionalization on the surface. Based on recent studies, we briefly reviewed the applications of CDs in advanced batteries.

When CDs are directly used as the anodes of LIBs and SIBs, their appropriate layer spacing can provide embedding sites for Li+ and Na+ and they can also adapt to the volume changes during insertion/extraction. Besides, the quantum size of CDs is beneficial to reduce the diffusion distance of Li+/Na+, so that the anode of CDs can deliver a high lithium/sodium storage capacity. In addition, their large specific surface area and rich oxygenic functional groups can contribute to the storage of lithium and sodium as well. When the surfaces of carbon-based materials, titanium-based materials, metal oxide/sulfide or other electrode materials are modified by graphite-type carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots, they can provide more electrochemical active sites, improve the conductivity of composite materials and improve the dynamics of ion diffusion and charge transfer. Meanwhile, the CQDs and GQDs can also be used as sensitizers and protective agents to prevent the rapid agglomeration of active materials, which can make the structure of the electrode more stable and further improve its cycling stability. CQDs can also be transformed into multidimensional carbon nanomaterials by direct calcination or catalysis at a high temperature. And it is of great importance to introduce P, S or N atoms in the self-assembly process of CDs for the formation and morphological evolution of carbon materials. Moreover, many other kinds of heteroatoms can also be introduced to provide more defects for carbon materials, which opens a new way for the design of multi-dimensional carbon materials. Furthermore, adding CDs into the synthesis system of metal or metal oxides/sulfides can not only improve the conductivity of electrode materials, CDs can also adjust their morphology and reduce the particle size, providing new possibilities for the design of high-capacity metal oxides/sulfides electrodes. In addition, CDs can effectively inhibit the dissolution and shuttle effect of polysulfide and provide nucleation locations for lithium metal, when they are used as the modifier of separators and the additives of electrolyte in Li-S batteries, which contributes to improving the utilization of sulfur and uniform lithium deposition to improve the cyclic stability.

Although CDs hold great promise in the application of batteries, they still face great challenges. At first, there is still a lack of more detailed discussion on CDs itself, including how to obtain high purity carbon dots with precise structure and how to realize simple and large-scale preparations of different types of CDs. The development of new controllable preparation technology of CDs with high purity, low cost and high yield is an important prerequisite to study the intrinsic properties of CDs and realize their large-scale applications. Secondly, CDs prepared by different methods have great differences in structure and performance, and they will have different effects on the modification and regulation of electrode materials. So, it is necessary to consider the size, crystal shape, surface status and miscellaneous modification of CDs prepared from different methods and make a strict classification, and the regulation and modification mechanism on electrode materials of different types of CDs are supposed to be studied in detail. So, the electrochemical properties of composite materials can be adjusted and predicted to lay a foundation for their practical applications. Finally, the applications of GQDs with detailed structural characteristics in electrode materials are very popular at present, and applications of other types of CDs in different components of batteries, including electrode materials, separators and electrolytes of batteries, need to be further explored. In this aspect, CDs with more functional groups have great potential to control the morphology of electrode materials to alleviate volume changes during circulation and accelerate ion and electron transmission, moreover, the rich functional groups in CDs are in favor of the application in electrolyte to adjust the ion migration.

In conclusion, CDs have shown promising application prospects in advanced batteries. Further research on large-scale preparation methods and systematic regulation mechanisms of carbon dots are the main directions for the practical applications of CDs in energy storage systems in the future.
